key_facts: Lorcan Tucker named permanent Ireland T20I captain
Appointment made ahead of Ireland's T20I series vs India
Tucker is Ireland's wicketkeeper-batter
Previously captained the side on interim basis

stories.headline: ICC rules that motherhood and cricket can coexist in landmark policy move
stories.what: The International Cricket Council has made a landmark declaration affirming that motherhood and professional cricket can coexist, signalling a major shift in how the global governing body approaches the welfare and careers of women cricketers. The move is widely seen as a response to growing calls for formal maternity protections and career support for female players who wish to start families without forfeiting their international careers. The ICC's stance brings cricket in line with other progressive sports governing bodies on the question of parental rights for athletes.
stories.lens: For every aspiring woman cricketer in India — a country rapidly growing its women's game — the ICC's formal stance on maternity means a professional career and motherhood are no longer mutually exclusive choices.
stories.article:
The International Cricket Council has taken a landmark step by formally affirming that motherhood and cricket can coexist, marking a watershed moment for women's cricket globally. The declaration, reported by India Today, signals a significant evolution in how the sport's governing body views the rights and careers of its female players.

Until now, women cricketers who became mothers have often faced an informal but real pressure to choose between family and career, with limited structural support from boards or the ICC itself. The new stance challenges that status quo directly, and is expected to push member boards — including the Board of Control for Cricket in India — to formalise their own maternity policies for contracted women players.

The move follows the lead of other major international sports bodies, including FIFA and the IOC, which have in recent years introduced explicit protections for pregnant athletes and new mothers. For cricket, whose women's game has grown enormously in stature, viewership and prize money over the past decade, the policy alignment is both overdue and consequential.

In the Indian context, the announcement carries particular weight. The BCCI has expanded its women's cricket infrastructure substantially, including the Women's Premier League, meaning more players now have professional contracts. An ICC-level commitment to maternity rights gives those players — and their boards — a clear framework to follow. It represents progress not just in welfare terms, but in the broader professionalisation of women's cricket worldwide.
key_facts: ICC declares motherhood and professional cricket can coexist
Marks a landmark policy shift for women cricketers globally
Move expected to pressure member boards to formalise maternity policies
Relevant to BCCI's growing pool of contracted women players

stories.headline: Did Amitabh Bachchan shelve Khabardar to avoid being overshadowed by Kamal Haasan?
stories.what: A long-circulating piece of Bollywood lore has resurfaced: that Amitabh Bachchan pulled out of — or effectively shelved — the film Khabardar because he feared being outshone by co-star Kamal Haasan. The Indian Express revisits the story behind the ill-fated collaboration between two of Hindi cinema's biggest names, a project that was never completed. The episode, if true, would represent a rare instance of Bollywood's biggest superstar stepping back from a project for fear of artistic competition.
stories.lens: The Bachchan-Haasan rivalry anecdote is a window into the ego politics and star-power calculations that have always shaped — and sometimes killed — ambitious Bollywood projects.
stories.article:
Decades after the fact, one of Bollywood's most tantalising 'what-ifs' is being revisited: did Amitabh Bachchan effectively kill the film Khabardar because he feared being outperformed by Kamal Haasan? The Indian Express digs into the rumour that has persisted in film circles for years, examining the circumstances that led to the project being shelved.

The story, as it has circulated in Bollywood lore, goes that Bachchan — then at the peak of his 'angry young man' superstardom — grew uneasy about sharing screen space with Kamal Haasan, widely regarded as one of the most technically gifted actors of his generation. Haasan's ability to transform physically and emotionally for roles was already becoming legendary, and the concern, reportedly, was that audiences might walk away talking more about him than the top-billed Bachchan.

Such dynamics were not uncommon in the Hindi film industry of that era. The star system placed enormous importance on hierarchy — who got more screen time, whose name appeared first, and crucially, who audiences went home remembering. A multi-starrer that upset that calculus could be commercially and reputationally damaging for the bigger name. Whether Bachchan's team made a deliberate call or circumstances conspired is something the report examines without definitive conclusion.

What makes the anecdote enduring is what it says about two parallel legends: Bachchan, the undisputed commercial king of Hindi cinema, and Haasan, the actor's actor who rarely played by Bollywood's star-system rules. A completed Khabardar would have been one of the most fascinating screen pairings of that era — and its absence remains one of Indian cinema's great unmade films.
key_facts: Film Khabardar was reportedly shelved after Amitabh Bachchan's exit
Kamal Haasan was co-star; Bachchan allegedly feared being overshadowed
Project represents one of Bollywood's most notable 'unmade films'

stories.headline: New OTT releases this week (June 22-28, 2026): 'Raja Shivaji', 'Gram Chikitsalay' Season 2, 'House of the Dragon' Season 3 and more
stories.what: This week's OTT slate (June 22–28, 2026) brings a packed lineup headlined by 'Raja Shivaji', the second season of the rural medical drama 'Gram Chikitsalay', and the long-awaited 'House of the Dragon' Season 3, among other titles across Indian and international platforms. The releases span multiple languages and genres, offering viewers a broad range of new content. It marks one of the more substantial weekly drops of the year for streaming audiences in India.
stories.lens: With House of the Dragon Season 3 and multiple Indian originals dropping simultaneously, Indian OTT subscribers have a genuinely strong week of new content to choose from across platforms.
stories.article:
Indian streaming audiences have a notably full plate this week, with new releases landing across platforms from June 22 to 28, 2026. The headliners include 'Raja Shivaji', a title likely to draw interest from Marathi and history enthusiasts; 'Gram Chikitsalay' Season 2, the returning rural healthcare drama that built a loyal following in its first season; and 'House of the Dragon' Season 3, the HBO fantasy series that remains one of the most-watched international shows among Indian OTT subscribers.

The Times of India's weekly OTT roundup highlights the breadth of the lineup, which spans Indian originals alongside major international titles. 'House of the Dragon', set in George R.R. Martin's Westeros universe as a prequel to Game of Thrones, returns after a season that ended on dramatic cliffhangers and will be available for Indian viewers on the platform that carries the HBO catalogue.

'Gram Chikitsalay' Season 2 continues the story of healthcare in rural India, a theme that resonated with audiences and critics during its debut season. Its return signals platform confidence in homegrown, regionally rooted storytelling as a viable content strategy.

For Indian viewers navigating multiple subscriptions, this week offers tangible value — a rare convergence of anticipated Indian originals and a globally tracked international franchise landing in the same seven-day window.
key_facts: House of the Dragon Season 3 releases this week on OTT
Gram Chikitsalay Season 2 returns for Indian streaming audiences
Raja Shivaji also part of the June 22–28 release slate
Releases span multiple platforms and genres

stories.headline: How Om Puri's craft silenced Raaj Kumar's on-set insults
stories.what: A behind-the-scenes anecdote about the late actors Om Puri and Raaj Kumar has resurfaced, describing how Puri's raw acting ability effectively stopped Raaj Kumar — notoriously imperious on film sets — from directing his trademark contempt at him. The story, revisited by The Indian Express, illustrates the complex hierarchies and personality clashes that defined Hindi cinema's golden era. It also serves as a tribute to Om Puri's formidable reputation as one of the most technically accomplished actors of his generation.
stories.lens: For fans of classic Hindi cinema, this anecdote captures something real about how genuine craft — not stardom — was the ultimate currency even in an industry driven by ego and hierarchy.
stories.article:
On the sets of Hindi cinema's golden era, few personalities were as intimidating as Raaj Kumar — an actor famed as much for his imperious manner and sharp tongue as for his screen presence. A story now doing the rounds, revisited by The Indian Express, describes how the late Om Puri managed to do what few could: render Raaj Kumar's disdain utterly ineffective, simply through the power of his acting.

The anecdote captures a moment where Raaj Kumar, known for belittling co-stars and crew, found himself unable to sustain his usual condescension in the face of Puri's extraordinary craft. Puri — trained at the Film and Television Institute of India (FTII) and the National School of Drama — brought an intensity to his performances that was virtually impossible to dismiss or diminish, even for someone of Raaj Kumar's formidable ego.

Om Puri's career was built on exactly this kind of quiet authority. From Ardh Satya to Jaane Bhi Do Yaaron, he repeatedly demonstrated that pure acting technique could command a room and a camera in ways that star power alone could not. The fact that even Raaj Kumar — no shrinking violet himself — had to concede that space is a telling measure of Puri's stature.

Both actors are now gone, but stories like these keep their legacies alive in vivid, human terms. The anecdote is less about rivalry and more about the unspoken grammar of respect on a film set — that ultimately, craft speaks loudest.
key_facts: Om Puri trained at FTII and National School of Drama
Raaj Kumar was known for his imperious, often dismissive manner on sets
Both actors are now deceased; anecdote revisits Hindi cinema's golden era

stories.headline: 'Ten-year-old Shah Rukh Khan would supply samosas to us at NSD': Pankaj Kapur
stories.what: Veteran actor Pankaj Kapur has shared a vivid childhood memory of Shah Rukh Khan from the National School of Drama (NSD), recalling how a ten-year-old SRK would supply samosas to those at the institution. The anecdote offers a rare, intimate glimpse into the superstar's early years in Delhi's theatre world, long before his Bollywood rise. Kapur's recollection underscores the deep personal connections formed at NSD that shaped Indian cinema's most celebrated careers.
stories.lens: A rare personal account placing Shah Rukh Khan inside India's most prestigious drama school as a child — a piece of Bollywood history that fans rarely get to hear directly from a contemporary.
stories.article:
Shah Rukh Khan's connection to the National School of Drama runs deeper than most fans know — and Pankaj Kapur has now put a charming, specific detail to it. The celebrated actor recalled that a ten-year-old SRK would supply samosas to people at the NSD, painting a vivid picture of a boy growing up on the fringes of India's most important theatre institution in Delhi.

Kapur, himself an NSD alumnus and one of Hindi cinema's most respected character actors, shared the anecdote in an interview with The Indian Express. The memory places Khan at NSD not as a student but as a child in and around the campus, absorbing the atmosphere that would later inform his craft — a detail that adds texture to the well-known story of his eventual Bollywood ascent from Delhi's theatre circuit.

NSD has long been the cradle of serious acting talent in India, producing luminaries ranging from Naseeruddin Shah and Om Puri to Irrfan Khan. Kapur's reminiscence situates Shah Rukh Khan within that lineage in a personal, human way — not as the global superstar he became, but as a samosa-supplying child watching greatness take shape around him.

The story resonates because it is precisely the kind of unguarded, off-the-record memory that rarely surfaces about stars of Khan's magnitude. Coming from Kapur, whose own artistic credibility is beyond question, it carries the weight of genuine recollection rather than promotional mythology.
key_facts: Pankaj Kapur recalls ten-year-old SRK supplying samosas at NSD
Anecdote shared in interview with The Indian Express
NSD, Delhi, is India's premier drama training institution
Kapur is himself an NSD alumnus and veteran Bollywood actor

stories.headline: Why Agent Vinod remains Sriram Raghavan's most fascinating failure
stories.what: The Indian Express revisits 'Agent Vinod', director Sriram Raghavan's 2012 spy thriller starring Saif Ali Khan, examining why the film — despite its ambition, craft, and Raghavan's signature genre sensibility — failed to land with audiences and critics. The piece positions the film as an instructive creative misfire from one of Hindi cinema's most distinctive directors, whose later works like 'Andhadhun' went on to become benchmarks of Indian thriller filmmaking. The retrospective analysis offers insight into what separates a compelling vision from a satisfying film.
stories.lens: For fans of Sriram Raghavan's later triumphs like Andhadhun, understanding where Agent Vinod fell short reveals how a filmmaker's craft sharpens — and why ambitious failures can be as revealing as hits.
stories.article:
Twelve years on, 'Agent Vinod' continues to fascinate — not for what it achieved, but for the gap between its ambition and its execution. Sriram Raghavan's 2012 spy thriller, starring Saif Ali Khan as a RAW agent navigating a globe-trotting mission, was a project of considerable scale and stylistic intention, yet it underperformed both critically and commercially in a way that still invites analysis.

The Indian Express takes a deep dive into why the film qualifies as Raghavan's 'most fascinating failure' — a phrase that itself says much. Raghavan, who had already demonstrated his noir instincts with 'Ek Haseen Thi' and would later deliver the near-universally acclaimed 'Andhadhun' (2018) and 'Merry Christmas' (2024), is a filmmaker with a clear and distinctive sensibility. That makes 'Agent Vinod' interesting precisely because the ingredients were present — a charismatic lead, an international canvas, genre affection — and yet the whole did not cohere.

The retrospective points to structural and tonal issues that plagued the film: a runtime that stretched its narrative thin, a Cold War-era spy aesthetic that felt more referential than alive, and a central performance that struggled to anchor the episodic plotting. Kareena Kapoor Khan co-starred in the film, and Pritam's music — particularly the title track — became arguably more enduring than the film itself.

For students of Indian cinema, the piece is a reminder that a director's journey is rarely linear. Raghavan's subsequent work suggests he absorbed the lessons of 'Agent Vinod' well — and that understanding a filmmaker's failures is often the most honest way to appreciate their eventual mastery.
key_facts: Agent Vinod (2012) directed by Sriram Raghavan, starring Saif Ali Khan
Film underperformed critically and commercially despite strong ambition
Raghavan later directed Andhadhun (2018), a major critical success
Indian Express retrospective examines the film's structural and tonal shortcomings
